votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq specification, including the frequency count, the constraints on the input list (non-empty and positive elements), and the identification of the maximum integer 'n' such that its frequency is at least 'n', or returning -1 if no such 'n' exists.

The Python code correctly implements the specification by checking the conditions on the list and finding the maximum valid n (which must be an element of the list since its frequency must be >= n > 0), returning -1 if no such n exists.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking that all elements are positive, the list is non-empty, and ans is either the maximum element n that appears at least n times, or -1 if no such element exists.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks all elements are positive, list is non-empty, finds all n > 0 where freq(n, lst) >= n, and returns the maximum such n if any exist (or -1 otherwise). The Coq spec requires ans to be the maximum valid n (via the condition that all valid n satisfy n <= ans), which matches Python's max(valid_ns). The Python only checks n in set(lst), which is correct since any n not in lst has freq 0 and cannot satisfy freq(n, lst) >= n for n > 0.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks all elements are positive, list is non-empty, finds all n > 0 where freq(n, lst) >= n, and returns the maximum such n if any exist, or -1 otherwise. The Coq spec requires ans to be the maximum valid n (via the condition that all valid n <= ans), which matches Python's max(valid_ns). The Python only checks n in set(lst), which is correct since any n not in lst has freq 0, so freq(n, lst) >= n with n > 0 is impossible.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: it checks all elements are positive, list is non-empty, finds all n > 0 where freq(n, lst) >= n, and returns the maximum such n (or -1 if none exist). The Coq spec requires ans to be the maximum valid n when valid ns exist (via the condition that all valid n <= ans), which matches Python's max(valid_ns). The Python only checks n in set(lst), which is correct since any n not in lst has freq 0, so freq(n, lst) >= n with n > 0 is impossible.

It checks all elements positive and non-empty, then if any n>0 with freq(n)>=n exists it requires ans be the maximal such n; otherwise ans=-1. Restricting to set(lst) is fine since any valid n must appear in the list. Matches the Coq spec on all inputs.

Both require lst to be non-empty and all positive; candidates n satisfy n>0 and freq(n,l)>=n, which implies n must be an element of lst. Coq’s ans is the maximal such n or -1 if none exist. Python computes exactly this (max over set(lst) with the condition, else -1). Thus results match on all concrete inputs.

Both compute freq by equality, require lst nonempty and all positive, select ans as the maximum positive n with freq(n) >= n if any exist, otherwise require ans = -1; restricting to n in set(lst) is sound since n must occur to satisfy freq(n) >= n.
